questions.question: Explain the difference between return by value, return by reference, and return by pointer in C++. When would you use each?
keyPoints: Return by value creates a copy of object using copy/move constructor
Return by reference returns alias to existing object, no copying
Return by pointer returns memory address, allows null
Use by value: when caller needs independent copy, RVO applies
Use by reference: for member access, operator overloading, avoid copying large objects
Use by pointer: when object might not exist (null), working with C-style APIs

questions.question: What is Return Value Optimization (RVO)? How does it affect performance when returning objects from functions?
keyPoints: RVO is a compiler optimization technique
Compiler constructs return value directly in caller's memory location
Eliminates copy and move constructor calls
Mandatory in C++17 for certain cases
Improves performance significantly for large objects
NRVO is similar but for named local variables

questions.question: What is a dangling reference? How can you avoid creating dangling references when returning from functions?
keyPoints: Dangling reference: reference to object whose lifetime has ended
Typically occurs when returning reference to local variable
To avoid: don't return reference to local automatic variables
Return by value for local objects
Return reference only to: static objects, members, or heap-allocated objects
Use std::move with smart pointers for ownership transfer

questions.question: Explain move semantics in C++11. How does std::move enable efficient object return from functions?
keyPoints: Move semantics transfers ownership without deep copying
std::move converts lvalue to rvalue reference
Enables move constructor instead of copy constructor
Useful for objects with dynamic resources (vectors, strings)
Reduces overhead when returning large objects
Source object left in valid but unspecified state

questions.question: What are the rules for returning *this from a member function? Explain with examples.
keyPoints: Return *this by value: returns copy of object
Return *this by reference: returns reference to same object
Used for operator chaining (operator<<, operator+=)
const member function: returns const reference
Enables fluent interface/chainable methods
Example: stream << obj1 << obj2 requires returning reference

questions.question: When should you return const reference? What are the advantages and limitations?
keyPoints: Return const reference when: returning member data, avoiding unnecessary copies
Advantage: no copy overhead, read-only access
Limitation: caller must ensure object lifetime exceeds their usage
Cannot use with temporary objects (except bound to const ref)
Use for getter methods returning member variables
Not suitable when caller needs independent copy

questions.question: What is copy elision? How does it differ from RVO?
keyPoints: Copy elision: compiler removing redundant copy/move operations
RVO is a specific form of copy elision for return values
Copy elision also applies to temporary objects in initializations
Mandatory copy elision in C++17 for certain cases
RVO/NRVO are optimizations (mandatory only for RVO in C++17)
Even without elision, move constructor may be used as fallback

questions.question: Design a class Complex with real and imaginary parts. Implement: (a) Constructor, (b) Copy constructor, (c) Move constructor, (d) Function to return magnitude (by value), (e) Function to return conjugate (by value), (f) Overload + operator to return by value. Explain the object return mechanisms used.
keyPoints: Class with private real, imag members
Constructor initializes values
Copy constructor prints message for demonstration
Move constructor uses initializer list for efficiency
magnitude() returns by value: sqrt(real*real + imag*imag)
conjugate() returns new Complex object by value
+ operator returns Complex result by value (enables chaining)
RVO applies to returned expressions
Move semantics used for returning local objects
Use of const correctness throughout

questions.question: Write a C++ program demonstrating three dangerous scenarios of returning references/pointers and explain how to fix each: (a) Returning reference to local variable, (b) Returning pointer to local variable, (c) Returning reference to heap-allocated memory without proper ownership.
keyPoints: Show code with each dangerous pattern
Explain why each is dangerous (dangling reference/pointer)
Fix for (a): return by value instead
Fix for (b): return by value or use smart pointer
Fix for (c): return smart pointer or document ownership transfer
Include complete corrected code for each case
Explain proper memory management principles
Show best practices with unique_ptr

questions.question: Explain with code examples how operator overloading for stream insertion (<<) and insertion (>>) should return values. Why is the return type important for chaining?
keyPoints: operator<< should return reference to ostream
operator>> should return reference to istream
Return type: ostream& for <<, istream& for >>
First parameter is stream reference, second is object
Returns stream reference to enable chaining
Example: cout << obj1 << obj2; parses as ((cout << obj1) << obj2)
If return void, chaining fails
If return by value, less efficient
Demonstrate with complete class example

questions.question: Create a complete C++ class String with dynamic memory allocation. Implement: (a) Constructor, (b) Copy constructor with deep copy, (c) Move constructor, (d) Overload + operator, (e) Function to return substring. Demonstrate with main() showing when copies vs moves occur.
keyPoints: String class with char* data member
Constructor allocates memory and copies string
Copy constructor: deep copy with new allocation
Move constructor: transfer pointer, set source to nullptr
+ operator: create new String from concatenated result
Substring function returns String by value
main() demonstrates: RVO, explicit copy, explicit move
Use std::move to force move semantics
Print statements to show constructor/copy/move calls
